coredata.dc:title: Alternanthera sessilis derived fluorescent carbon dots and their sensing and biological applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Bijimol, D.
Mathew, Jincy
Plathanam, Neena John
George, Bini
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: Herein, the green synthesis of novel fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) from the Alternanthera sessilis plant by a simple, eco-friendly, and facile hydrothermal method was reported. The developed CDs exhibit green fluorescence and have a size of 5.25 nm. Various analyses reveal outstanding features, including high quantum yield, good photostability, and excellent hydrophilicity of the as-synthesized CDs. It was used as a dual-mode probe for the sensing of Cu2+ ions and Zn2+ ions in an aqueous system. Efficient sensing of Cu2+ ions takes place by static quenching of fluorescence with a detection limit of 28.03 nM. CDs-coated glassy carbon electrode was used to perform the electrochemical detection of Zn2+ ions in an aqueous system with high selectivity and sensitivity, and the LOD value was found to be 2.28 nM. The LC50 value of the fabricated CDs against the human breast cancer cell line is 89.85 µL/mL. Also, the antimicrobial efficacy of the CDs was examined against Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus Aureus, and Aspergillus niger, and the results indicate the high toxicity of the CDs against all tested microbial strains.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Water pollution
Fluorescent sensor
Quenching efficiency
Electrochemical sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dual mode sensing of Cu2+ ions and Zn2+ ions in natural water samples
Analysis of the structural and morphological characteristics of CDs
Analysis of the stability of CDs
Analysis of the selectivity, interference, and effect of pH
Fluorescent quenching mechanism of Cu2+ ions
Electrochemical detection of Zn2+ ions by CD/GCE
Sensitivity study by differential pulse voltammetric method
Mechanism of the electrochemical detection of Zn2+ ions
Analysis of real samples via fluorescent and electrochemical methods

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Images of (a) TEM, (b) HR-TEM, (c) SAED pattern, and (d) particle size distribution of CDs.
(a) FT-IR, (b) Raman, and (c) XRD spectra of the synthesized CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, and (d) O 1 s of CDs.
(a) Absorption (b) excitation and emission spectra of CDs, and (c) variation of the emission spectrum of CDs with different excitation wavelengths.
Effect of (a) pH, (b) ionic strength, (c) UV light, (d) storage time, and (e) temperature on the stability of CDs.

(a) Zeta potential analysis of CDs, (b) fluorescence behavior of CDs with various metal ions, (c) interference study of fluorescence sensing of Cu2+ ions with CDs at pH 7, and (d) quenching efficiency of CDs under different pH.
(a) Sensitivity study of CDs with Cu2+ ions up to the concentration of 0.60 µM, and (b) calibration plot with I0/I vs. the concentration of Cu2+ ions.
TEM images of (a) CDs, and (b) CDs/Cu2+ ions system.

Zeta potential values of CDs (a) in the absence and (b) presence of Cu2+ ions, (c) FT-IR spectra of CDs and CDs / Cu2+ ions system, and (d) time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of CDs and CDs / Cu2+ ions system.
(a) CV plots of unmodified GCE, CD modified GCE without and with Zn2+ ions, (b) pH study, (c) influence of scan rate, and (d) scan rate and peak current correlation.
(a) DPV curves of CD/GCE with varying Zn2+ ions concentrations, and (b) the calibration plot showing the linear relationship of peak current vs. concentrations of Zn2+ ions.
(a) Selectivity study, (b) stability analysis, and (c) electrochemical impedance spectra of CD/GCE.
Morphological changes induced by the fabricated CDs on the MCF-7 (breast cancer cell line).
Morphological changes induced by the fabricated CDs on the L929 (Mouse Fibroblast cell line).
Concentration-dependent cytotoxic effect of the CDs against (a) breast carcinoma cell line, MCF-7, and (b) Mouse Fibroblast cell line, L929.
Antibacterial potency of the fabricated CDs with Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus.
Antifungal activity of the CDs against Aspergillus niger.
Hydrothermal synthesis of CDs.
Mechanism of electrochemical sensing of Zn2+ ions via CD/GCE.
Elemental composition of CDs.
A comparative analysis of the detection of Cu2+ ions in water via fluorescent method.
Comparison of the efficiencies of various sensors for the detection of Zn2+ ions by electrochemical method.
Real sample analysis for the fluorescent detection of Cu2+ ions in water (n = 3).
Real sample analysis of the electrochemical detection of Zn2+ ions water.
Antibacterial efficacy of the CDs.
Antifungal activity of CDs.

head.ce:title: Alternanthera sessilis derived fluorescent carbon dots and their sensing and biological applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam 686560, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam 686560, Kerala, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical Sciences, Central University of Kerala, Kasaragod 671316, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical Sciences, Central University of Kerala, Kasaragod 671316, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, the green synthesis of novel fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) from the Alternanthera sessilis plant by a simple, eco-friendly, and facile hydrothermal method was reported. The developed CDs exhibit green fluorescence and have a size of 5.25 nm. Various analyses reveal outstanding features, including high quantum yield, good photostability, and excellent hydrophilicity of the as-synthesized CDs. It was used as a dual-mode probe for the sensing of Cu2+ ions and Zn2+ ions in an aqueous system. Efficient sensing of Cu2+ ions takes place by static quenching of fluorescence with a detection limit of 28.03 nM. CDs-coated glassy carbon electrode was used to perform the electrochemical detection of Zn2+ ions in an aqueous system with high selectivity and sensitivity, and the LOD value was found to be 2.28 nM. The LC50 value of the fabricated CDs against the human breast cancer cell line is 89.85 µL/mL. Also, the antimicrobial efficacy of the CDs was examined against Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus Aureus, and Aspergillus niger, and the results indicate the high toxicity of the CDs against all tested microbial strains.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are new-generation carbon nanomaterials of zero dimension with a size of less than 20 nm [1]. The scientific community is very interested in their research due to their unique properties like high photostability [2], enormous sources [3], excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity [4], and easy surface functionalization [5]. These peculiar properties help the CDs to have enormous applications in various fields like sensing [6], photocatalysis [7], bioimaging [5], drug delivery [8], electrocatalysis [9], etc.

The carbon dots have been synthesized by various methods like sonication, hydrothermal, solvothermal, laser ablation, arc discharge, pyrolysis, microwave irradiation, carbonization, etc. [10]. The hydrothermal approach is a facile, low-cost, eco-friendly, and non-toxic route for preparing CDs using both natural and chemical precursors [11,12]. Natural resources can be used to prepare CDs because of the large amount of cellulose, flavonoids, amino acids, proteins, etc. Their advantages include low cost, easy availability, self-surface passivating ability, and provision of heteroatoms. As given in the literature, during the carbonization, the heteroatoms in biomass move to the surface of CDs [13]. However, nitrogen doping alone usually occurs in the majority of syntheses [14,15]. The various properties and, hence, the applications of CDs highly depend on the selection of the precursor. Nowadays, a large number of natural precursors like lemon juice [16], sweet potatoes [17], cucumber peel [18], garlic [19], lotus roots [20], and jackfruit seed [21] are chosen for the preparation of CDs through the simple and facile hydrothermal approach. With the use of these CDs, hazardous metal ions, drugs, pesticides, and other harmful contaminants can be found in the aqueous medium with extreme sensitivity and selectivity [22].

Heavy metals are essential for the smooth functioning of various biological processes. The use of heavy metals in different industries leads to their accumulation into the environment and water resources, and their concentration exceeds the permissible limit [23–25]. Copper is a transition metal and has a significant role in different physiological processes in living organisms. However, excess concentration of copper ions may result in oxidative stress in the liver and cause various neurodegenerative diseases like Wilson’s disease and Alzheimer’s disease [26,27]. The maximum allowed level of copper ions in drinking water is 1.3 ppm, or 20 µM, according to the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [28]. Zinc metal is the major constituent of various enzymes and proteins in the biological systems [29] and has significant physiological functions in the human body [30]. Zinc has enormous applications in multiple fields, including galvanization, paints, pharmaceuticals, batteries, and alloy manufacturing, which leads to its easy arrival into the ecosystem [31]. The poor biodegradation of zinc may result in accumulation and can cause soil and water resources to become polluted. The World Health Organization (WHO) recommends 5.0 mg/L as the upper limit for zinc in drinking water [32]. A dose higher than this causes serious health problems like gastroenteritis, high fever, heavy weight loss, etc. [33].

Even though a number of analytical techniques are used for the detection of metal ions [34–38], some of them require sophisticated instruments, expert technicians, and long-term analysis. Hence, developing a simple, economical, and efficient sensor for detecting metal ions is highly desirable. Due to their excellent selectivity and sensitivity, fluorescent spectroscopy and electrochemical methods are frequently employed for the sensing of metal ions.

In this study, the simple hydrothermal approach was used to produce green fluorescent CDs using the Alternanthera sessilis plant. The common names of this plant are joy weed or noxious weed. It is used as a vegetable and has several medicinal properties. The components include fats, proteins, carbohydrates, starch, amino acids, calcium, phosphorous, and vitamins A, B1, B2, and C. The medicinal properties include anticancer, antiulcer, antipyretic, antioxidant, antifungal, and antibacterial properties [39]. Here, the fabricated CDs were used as the fluorescent sensor of Cu2+ ions and electrochemical sensor of Zn2+ ions in addition to their anticancer, antibacterial, and antifungal properties. Real sample analysis was also carried out effectively with the developed sensor.

Alternanthera sessilis plants were gathered from the nearby land. Disodium hydrogen phosphate, monosodium hydrogen phosphate, quinine sulfate, K2Cr2O7, MnCl2·4H2O, CrCl3, Pb(NO3)2, NiCl2·6H2O, CdCl2·H2O, HgCl2, CoCl2·6H2O, ZnSO4·7H2O, CuSO4·5H2O,AlCl3, CaCl2,BaCl2,Ce(NO3)3 KCl, AgCl and NaCl were purchased from Merck, India. Double distilled water was used throughout the entire analysis.

Perkin-Elmer 400 FT-IR spectrometer was used for the recording of the FT-IR spectrum of the sample in the range 4000–400 cm−1. UV–vis. analysis was performed using a Shimadzu 3600 Plus UV–vis. spectrometer. Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrofluorometer was used for fluorescent analysis. XRD analysis was carried out on a Bruker AXS D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer. A JEOL-JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope acquired the morphological analysis using the transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images. The Horiba SZ-100 scientific nanoparticle analyzer was used to perform both the particle size analysis and the zeta potential measurement. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies were carried out with a Physical Electronics apparatus. The electrochemical study was performed using a Biologic SP-300 electrochemical workstation.

Alternanthera sessilis plants were used to prepare fluorescent CDs through the simple hydrothermal method. The collected rootless plants were cleaned well using distilled water and dried under shade. In the subsequent step, 1.75 g of the leaf powder was combined with 75 mL of distilled water in a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 6 h and allowed the autoclave to cool naturally to room temperature after the allotted time. Following the removal of any remaining particles, the brown solution was centrifuged for 15 min at 10000 rpm before being filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. A dialysis membrane (1 K MWCO) was used to purify the fabricated CDs in distilled water for 2–3 days and kept at 4 °C for further analysis. Scheme 1 demonstrates the preparation method of CDs.

The fluorescent examination was done in an aqueous solution to investigate the developed sensor's selectivity towards Cu2 + ions among other metal ions. The metal ions, including Zn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cr6+, Na+, K+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Sr2+ Mg2+, Ag+, Ce3+, and Hg2+were used for the analysis. All the experiments were conducted at pH 7 and at room temperature. 200 μL of various metal salt solutions were mixed with 50 μL of the CDs and 2.5 mL of the phosphate buffer solution in different test tubes and ultrasonicated for 5 min. The fluorescent intensities of the samples were recorded, and the results revealed the high selectivity of the CDs towards Cu2+ ions. The sensitivity study of the proposed sensor for detecting Cu2+ ions was performed by recording the emission intensities of the solutions of CDs containing various concentrations of Cu2+ ions under the same experimental conditions. The interference analysis was conducted by measuring the fluorescent intensities of a mixture containing the same concentrations of each interfering ion and the Cu2+ ions, 50 μL CDs, and 2.5 mL PBS of pH 7. The PL intensities of the CDs with and without the metal ions are denoted as I and I0, respectively.

The three-electrode method was used for the electrochemical detection of Zn2+ ions. Here, the reference electrode was an Ag/AgCl electrode, the counter electrode was a Pt wire, and the working electrode was a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). The GCE was initially polished using a 0.05 µM alumina slurry. With the aid of cyclic voltammetric and differential pulse voltammetric analyses, the CD-modified GCE (CD/GCE) was created by the drop casting method and employed for the selectivity and sensitivity studies of the sensor at pH 6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual mode sensing of Cu2+ ions and Zn2+ ions in natural water samples
ce:section.ce:para: River and tap water were used to test the proposed sensor's applicability. The collected water samples were centrifuged and filtered by a porous membrane of thickness 0.22 µm to eliminate any suspended impurities. Three separate solutions of known concentration were prepared using the real samples by spiking them with known amounts of Cu2+ ions and Zn2+ ions. Then, the fluorescent and electrochemical analyses were conducted on these samples, respectively.

An MTT assay was used to assess the anticancer efficacy of the synthesized CDs against the human breast cancer cell line (MCF-7). The MCF-7 cells were maintained in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium, enriched with a solution consisting of 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin and streptomycin, amphotericin B, L-glutamate, and sodium bicarbonate. The cell suspension was dispersed in 96-well plates and maintained in an incubator at 37 °C with 5 % CO2.

When sufficient growth was achieved, various concentrations of CDs were added to the plates and incubated for 24 h. The cells without CDs serve as the control. After the incubation, the images were obtained through an inverted phase contrast tissue culture microscope. After washing the sample content with phosphate buffer solution, 30 μL dilute MTT reagent was added to each well and kept at 37 °C for 4 h in the CO2 incubator. After the removal of the MTT solution, DMSO was added to the well for the dissolution of formazan crystals. Using the microplate reader, the absorbance of each well was noted at 540 nm. The whole analysis was triplicated, and the result was expressed as the cell viability percentage. The percentage of cell viability is calculated using the equation given below.
Cell viability
%
=
Average absorbance of the sample
/
Average absorbance of the control
× 1 00
The percentage of growth inhibition was calculated as
Growth inhibition
%
=
1 00 -
Cell viability
%
The LD50 value of the sample against the MCF-7 cell line was found using the software ED50plus v1.0.

The antibacterial efficacy of the synthesized carbon dots towards the bacterial strains Escherichia coli. and Staphylococcus aureus was analyzed using the agar well diffusion method. The bacterial strains were dispersed in 20 mL Muller Hinton agar medium on different Petri plates. Then, various sample volumes like 25 µL, 50 µL, and 100 µL were added to separate wells of a diameter of 10 mm in the Petri dish and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The size of the inhibition zone surrounding the well is directly proportional to the antibacterial efficacy of the sample. Here, the positive control is streptomycin with a concentration of 10 µg/mL.

The agar well diffusion method was used to determine the antifungal potency of the fabricated CDs, hence exploring their biological significance. The fungal strain Aspergillus niger was used for the study and swabbed overnight in potato dextrose agar plates. To the wells of about 10 mm diameter made in the Petri dishes, varying concentrations of the prepared CDs were mixed and incubated for 24 h. The antifungal potency of the CDs was studied by measuring the size of the area of inhibition around the wells at room temperature. The positive control was clotrimazole (10 µg/mL).
The CDs were synthesized from the plant Alternanthera sessilis using the simple hydrothermal approach. The fabricated CDs were characterized by a series of analytical methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the structural and morphological characteristics of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The particle size and shape of the produced CDs were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). They have a roughly spherical shape and a uniform dispersion without aggregation, as seen in Fig. 1 a. With an average size of 5.25 nm, the particle size ranges from 3.12 to 7.96 nm. The obtained fringe width value of 0.22 nm is extremely close to the interplanar spacing of the (002) plane in the graphitic structure, and the lattice fringes were clearly visible in the HR-TEM image (Fig. 1b) [40]. The amorphous character of the produced CDs is indicated by the wide circular ring in the SAED pattern in Fig. 1c. The particle size distribution of the produced CDs is displayed in Fig. 1d.

The functional groups in the developed CDs were identified by FT-IR analysis and XPS elemental analysis. In Fig. 2 a, The OH and NH stretching vibrations are represented by the broad and intense absorption band at 3282 cm−1. The band at 2961 cm−1 is of the stretching vibrations of the C-H bonds. The CO stretching vibrations are responsible for the strong band at 1638 cm−1. The bending vibrations of the CN bonds are accountable for the absorption band at 1438 cm−1. The NH bending vibration band appears at 1379 cm−1. The C-N bending vibrations are the origin of the band at 1113 cm−1 [41].

Two broad peaks at 1341 and 1565 cm−1 are seen in the Raman spectra of the CDs (Fig. 2b). The vibrations of the carbon atoms in the disordered graphitic structure gave rise to the first band, the D band. As a result, it is often referred to as the disorder band or defect band. The second band is the G band, which corresponds to the E2g vibrations of carbon atoms in the graphene structure [42,43]. The ID/IG ratio can be used to determine the degree of disorder in the graphitic structure where ID and IG are, respectively, the peak intensities of the D and G bands. The G band has a higher intensity than D band, and the calculated value of the ID/IG ratio is 0.77, indicating a highly ordered arrangement of graphitic carbon atoms with some disordered sp3 hybridized carbon sites in the developed CDs [44]. A broad peak at 23.06 0 in the XRD pattern (Fig. 2c) reveals the amorphous nature of the synthesized CDs.

The high water solubility, good photostability, and fluorescence properties of CDs' are attributed to the various surface functionalities, such as hydroxyl and amino groups, in the fabricated CDs. The results of FT-IR and XPS analyses are in good agreement, confirming the existence of functional groups, including oxygen and carbon. Because of their unique properties, CDs can have a variety of applications.

The optical properties of the synthesized CDs were analyzed via. UV–vis. and fluorescence spectroscopic measurements. The absorption and emission spectra of the CDs are shown in Fig. 4 a. The absorption spectrum showed two distinct bands at 212 and 280 nm, which were identified as the π-π* and n-π* transitions of the CC and CO bonds, respectively. The maximum emission in the fluorescence spectrum is noted at 435 nm with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Fig. 4b shows the PL emission spectra at various excitation wavelengths up to 390 nm. As shown in the graph, the developed CDs have an excitation-dependent emission character. Also, a red shift is observed in the emission spectrum with a rise in excitation wavelength. The variation in particle size and surface state of the synthesized CDs are responsible for these properties [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the stability of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the fabricated CDs can be analyzed by measuring the fluorescence intensities under various pH conditions, storage time, ionic strengths, temperatue, and UV light irradiation. As in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence stability remains almost constant up to pH 8 and thereafter decreases with pH. Fig. 5b depicts the influence of various ionic strengths on the PL intensity of the developed CDs. No remarkable change in fluorescent intensity is observed with NaCl concentrations in the range of 0.0–1.0 M. Due to their excellent photostability, the synthesized CDs retain their fluorescent power without apparent change even after the continuous irradiation of UV light for 60 min, as shown in Fig. 5c. The storage ability of the CDs is shown in Fig. 5d. No significant difference in the PL intensities was noticed even after forty days. As depicted in Fig. 5e the PL intensity of CDs decreases with increase in temperature. All these observations reveal the applicability of developed CDs for various purposes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the selectivity, interference, and effect of pH
ce:section.ce:para: As clear from Fig. 6 a, the negative value of zeta potential confirms the existence of negatively charged surface functionalities in the CDs, which can interact with the positively charged metal ions, thereby changing their PL Intensity. This interaction between the various metal ions and the CDs was investigated by measuring their fluorescence intensities. The selectivity study was conducted after mixing 0.2 mL (1 mM) solutions of different metal ions with 2.5 mL CDs solution. Fig. 6b depicts the selectivity analysis of the CDs and the different metal ions. It was found that while all the other metal ions show no or little quenching, Cu2+ ions cause significant quenching on the fluorescence of the prepared CDs. The interference study of various metal ions on the fluorescence sensing of Cu2+ ions was also conducted and displayed in Fig. 6c. It is observed that the quenching is not affected by the interfering analytes. The results reveal the excellent selectivity of the developed sensor towards the fluorescence detection of Cu2+ ions.

The sensing applications of CDs are extremely pH-dependent. Hence the influence of pH (3–11) on the fluorescence sensing of Cu2+ ions by the synthesized CDs was also examined. The highest quenching efficiency was attained at pH 7, as indicated in Fig. 6d. As a result, it was chosen as the ideal pH for the entire analysis.

The fluorescent intensity of the CDs was changed with the addition of different concentrations of the analyte. The Stern-Volmer equation given below was used to analyze the relationship between the concentration of the analyte and the PL intensity of CDs.
(1)
I 0
/ I = 1 +
Ksv
Q
Here, I and I0 stand for the fluorescence intensities with and without Cu2+ ions, [Q] represents the analyte concentration, and Ksv is the quenching constant. The sensitivity study of CDs towards Cu2+ ions in the concentration range of 0–60 µM is shown in Fig. 7 a. The calibration graph is plotted with I0/I values against Cu2+ ions concentration (Fig. 7b) and having a linear relationship in the range 0.05–0.45 µM and the correlation coefficient (R2) value was 0.9959.

The formula used to determine the limit of detection (LOD) is
(2)
LOD = 3 σ / S
where σ and S stand for the standard deviation and slope, respectively. The LOD value in this case is 28.03 nM, which is lower than the allowed concentration of Cu2+ ions in drinking water suggested by theWorld Health Organization(WHO). The above results demonstratethe suitability of the proposedfluorescent sensor for detecting the Cu2+ ions in environmental water samples. In Table 2 , the outcomes of the current investigation and other published studies for the fluorescence sensing of Cu2+ ions are compared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent quenching mechanism of Cu2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The results of the various spectroscopic analyses of the developed CDs confirmed the existence of various reactive surface groups like amino and carboxyl which leads to possible interaction with the analytes. Sometimes, this interaction causes CDs to assemble, which causes fluorescence quenching. The clustering of CDs upon the addition of Cu2+ ions was confirmed by the TEM images of CDs with and without the Cu2+ ions, as shown in Fig. 8 a and Fig. 8b. In this case, the analyte is the Cu2+ ion, and its complexation with the surface functional groups of the CDs results in the splitting of the d orbitals of the Cu2+ ion [46]. The difference in the zeta potential values of the CDs, as in Fig. 9 a and Fig. 9b, was attributed to their interaction with the analyte, Cu2+ ions. The interaction between CDs and Cu2+ ions was confirmed from the FT-IR spectra of pure CDs and CDs/ Cu2+ ions(Fig. 9c) The quenching mechanism involves the nonradiative electron transfer from the negatively charged surface groups to the d orbitals of the Cu2+ ions [47]. The fluorescence lifetime measurements were also conducted to understand the quenching mechanism further. As shown in Fig. 9d, the PL lifetime of pure CDs was 5.80 ns, while that of the CDs /Cu2+ ions system was 5.69 ns. The fluorescent quenching may be static because there are no significant changes in PL lifetime values on adding Cu2+ ions.

The suggested mechanism can also explain the variation of the quenching efficiency with pH, as shown in Fig. 6d. At low pH values, only a few negatively charged groups are available for bonding with Cu2+ ions. When the pH value increases, more negative charges are present due to the polarization of the surface moieties, which can easily bind the Cu2+ ions, and hence the quenching efficiency increases. But at very high pH values, the hydroxide ions and negatively charged surface groups compete for binding with Cu2+ ions, leading to decreased fluorescence quenching efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of Zn2+ ions by CD/GCE

ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the electrochemical performance of the CDs modified glassy carbon electrode (CD/GCE), several electrochemical techniques, including cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), and electrochemical impedance spectrum analysis, were employed. The modified and unmodified electrodes were subjected to cyclic voltammetric measurements at a potential range of 0–0.7 V with a scan rate of 50 mV/s. The current response of CD/GCE is higher than that of bare GCE, as seen in Fig. 10 a. The surface of CDs-modified GCE becomes more conductive in the presence of Zn2+ ions, producing two peaks that stand for the oxidation and reduction processes.

The impact of pH on the electrochemical sensing of Zn2+ ions was investigated in the pH range of 3 to 9. The highest current response was noticed at a pH of 6, as indicated in Fig. 10b. Therefore, pH 6 was chosen as the ideal pH to conduct further electrochemical studies. CV analysis was carried out at various scan rates ranging from 10 to 100 mV/s to assess the impact of scanning rate on the effectiveness of CD/GCE. The positive relationship between the peak current and the scan rate can be seen in the cyclic voltammograms in Fig. 10c. This process is reversible in nature because the peak position remained unchanged. Fig. 10d provides evidence that the current and scan rates have a linear relationship.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity study by differential pulse voltammetric method
ce:section.ce:para: DPV analysis was used to determine the impact of concentration on the electrochemical sensing of Zn2+ ions by the CD/GCE. It was carried out at pH 6 with a fixed scan rate of 50 mV/s and Zn2+ ion concentrations of 10–60 nM. As seen in Fig. 11 a, the peak current intensity rises with the Zn2+ ion concentration. The linear relationship between the current response and Zn2+ ion concentrations can also be seen in the calibration plot (Fig. 11b). The suggested electrochemical sensor has a linear range of 10–60 nM and an extremely low LOD value of 2.28 nM. A comparison of the studies relating to the electrochemical detection of Zn2+ ions is given in Table 3 .

The selectivity of the novel CD/GCE for the electrochemical sensing of Zn2+ ions was conducted using CV analysis. As displayed in Fig. 12 a, the CD/GCE has a clear electrochemical response for Zn2+ ions among various metal ions. By using CV analysis, the repeatability and stability of the proposed sensor were examined. The CV plots of the freshly modified electrode and the electrode after a period of one month were recorded (Fig. 12b) in the voltage range of −0.1–0.8 V, and the two peak current intensities did not significantly differ. This demonstrates the sensor's superb stability.

The electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) depicted in Fig. 12c was used to further evaluate the sensor's performance and displayed the sensor’s resistance as a semicircle. As observed, modified GCE has lesser resistance than the bare GCE. This results in the higher conductance and fast electron transfer response rate of the proposed sensor. All the results support the excellent performance of the CD/GCE as an electrochemical sensor for Zn2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the electrochemical detection of Zn2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The CV graph of the CD/GCE/Zn2+ system consists of two distinct peaks corresponding to the redox reaction of the Zn2+/Zn system at the electrode surface. The redox reaction occurs due the electron transfer process between the analyte and the surface functionalities of the CDs. The positive peak indicates the oxidation of the zinc atom to Zn2+ ions, and the reverse reaction (reduction of Zn2+ ions to the zinc atom) leads to the appearance of a negative peak. This redox reaction helps in the electrochemical detection of Zn2+ ions at the surface of CD/GCE. Scheme 2 illustrates a potential electrochemical sensing technique for Zn2+ ions using a CD-modified GCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of real samples via fluorescent and electrochemical methods
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the effectiveness of the manufactured CDs as a fluorescence sensor for Cu2+ ions and an electrochemical sensor for Zn2+ ion detection in the aqueous system, real sample analysis was done. The fluorescence intensities were evaluated after various known amounts of Cu2+ ions were introduced to the real samples (river and tap water). The obtained results were summed up in Table 4 . For the electrochemical detection of Zn2+ ions, the DPV analysis was done by measuring the current value of the sensor with and without the analyte, Zn2+ ions. The results were tabulated in Table 5 . The low relative standard deviation values and the good recovery rates in both methods reveal the suitability of the suggested sensor for the investigation of environmental water samples with high accuracy.

The anticancer effects of the synthesized CDs against the human breast cancer cell MCF-7 were evaluated using the MTT test. The morphological changes brought about by the addition of various CD concentrations to the MCF-7 human breast carcinoma cell were noted. The cytotoxicity of the CDs was revealed by the changes in the morphology of the cells like shrinkage, rounding, aggregation, etc., as in Fig. 13 . As is evident from Fig. 14 , the MCF-7 breast cancer cell line's cell viability is inversely proportional to the rise in CD concentration. Some studies reported that the cytotoxic effect of the nanoparticles might be due to their ability to produce reactive oxygen species capable of destroying the cellular component through intracellular oxidative stress, which finally reaches cell death [54]. The LD50 value of the fabricated CDs was 89.85 µl/mL, which is much smaller than that of other materials. To assess the clinical application of the synthesized CDs as anticancer agents, the cytotoxic analysis was carried out against normal cell, L929 (Mouse Fibroblast cell line) and the results reveal the low cytotoxicity and good cell viability of the synthesized CDs with a LD50 value of 285.55 µL/mL. The results indicate the significance of fabricated CDs in cancer therapy. (Fig. 15. )

The Agar well diffusion method was used to assess the antibacterial efficacy of theproduced CDs against Escherichia coli (gram-positive) and Staphylococcus aureus (gram-negative) bacterial strains. Fig. 16 provides images ofthe antibacterial effectiveness of CDs towards both bacteria. The size of the inhibition zone formed around the well indicates the antibacterial potency of the synthesized CDs. Because of the availability of many functional groups, CDs can effectively bind to the sulfur and phosphorous groups in the bacterial cell wall, leading to DNA damage and protein dysfunction [55] leading to the ultimate destruction of microorganisms.

Table 6 displays the values of inhibition zones for both bacteria. It was clear from the results that the antibacterial property of the CDs is higher for gram-negative bacteria than for gram-positive bacteria. The stability of gram-positive bacteria may be explained in terms of the thick peptidoglycan layer present in their cell wall, which restricts the entry of the CDs through the cell membrane.

The antifungal property of the developed CDs was studied using the Agar well diffusion approach. The fungal stain used was Aspergillus niger, and the obtained results are displayed in Fig. I7. . As explained in the antibacterial study here, the CDs can strongly interact with the various sulfur and phosphorous containing groups in the cell wall, causing their dysfunction and cell death. The zone of inhibition values are displayed in Table 7 .

In this chapter, novel, highly fluorescent CDs were synthesized using the Alternanthera sessilis plant as the precursor via a simple and cost-effective hydrothermal method. Various analytical techniques were used to examine the properties of the fabricated CDs. The developed CDs have high stability under different conditions and serve as highly efficient and selective fluorescent probe for Cu2+ ions detection and an electrochemical sensor for Zn2+ ions in aqueous resources with very low LOD values of 28.03 nM and 2.28 nM, respectively. The practical utility of the CDs was examined with tap and river water samples. This study reveals the excellent anticancer activity of the CDs against breast cancer cell, MCF-7 with an LD50 value of 89.85 µl/mL. The LD50 value obtained for normal cell line is 285.55 µL/mL which suggests the use of fabricated CDs as potential anticancer agents. It also exploits the antimicrobial efficacy of the developed CDs against the bacterial strains Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus and the fungal strain Aspergillus niger. The inhibition zone diameter varies with microbial strains, and the value is highest for gram-negative bacteria, Escherichia coli.

D. Bijimol: Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Resources, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Visualization. Jincy Mathew: Visualization, Data curation. Neena John Plathanam: Data curation, Visualization. Bini George: Visualization, Data curation. Beena Mathew: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from UGC HRDC, Govt.of India and DST-PURSE Phase II from the Department of Science & Technology (DST), Govt. of India.
